abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), which integrate vision and other modalities into Large Language Models (LLMs), significantly enhance AI capabilities but also introduce new security vulnerabilities. By exploiting the vulnerabilities of the visual modality and the long-tail distribution characteristic of code training data, we present PiCo, a novel jailbreaking framework designed to progressively bypass multi-tiered defense mechanisms in advanced MLLMs. PiCo employs a tier-by-tier jailbreak strategy, using token-level typographic attacks to evade input filtering and embedding harmful intent within programming context instructions to bypass runtime monitoring. To comprehensively assess the impact of attacks, a new evaluation metric is further proposed to assess both the toxicity and helpfulness of model outputs post-attack. By embedding harmful intent within code-style visual instructions, PiCo achieves an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 84.13% on Gemini-Pro Vision and 52.66% on GPT-4, surpassing previous methods. Experimental results highlight the critical gaps in current defenses, underscoring the need for more robust strategies to secure advanced MLLMs.

keyInsights: MLLMs exhibit latent vulnerabilities in their multimodal integration, particularly when processing fragmented visual information combined with specific, structured contextual cues.
The extensive training of MLLMs on code data creates a specific blind spot, making them susceptible to malicious instructions disguised as programming tasks.
The synergistic effect of fragmented visual text and code-style contextualization is crucial for effectively bypassing the multi-tiered defense mechanisms of modern MLLMs.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) introduce new security vulnerabilities due to their integration of visual and textual inputs, expanding the attack surface beyond traditional LLMs.
Existing MLLM red-teaming methods, such as converting harmful content into images, are not sufficiently robust against advanced, closed-source models and their multi-tiered defense mechanisms.
Current safety alignments in MLLMs often fail to address latent weaknesses in the integration of multimodal inputs and biases from the long-tail distribution of code training data.
results: PiCo achieved an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 84.13% on Gemini-Pro Vision and 52.66% on GPT-4o, outperforming "Text-only" and HADES baselines by substantial margins (e.g., +44.93% on GPT-4o).

The newly introduced Toxicity and Helpfulness Score (THS) confirmed that PiCo's jailbroken outputs were not only successful in bypassing defenses but also effectively toxic and helpful for malicious intent (e.g., Gemini Pro THS increased from 0.36 to 0.63).

PiCo demonstrated resilience against state-of-the-art defense methods like Self Reminder and Dynamic Defense Prompting, maintaining high success rates (e.g., 54.00% ASR in 'Violence' on GPT-4o with DDP), indicating deeper vulnerabilities beyond prompt-based mitigations.
solution: PiCo employs a multi-tiered jailbreak strategy to bypass access control, input filtering, and runtime monitoring in MLLMs.

It utilizes a "token-level typographic attack" where harmful instructions are fragmented into visually coherent but semantically discontinuous components within an image to evade keyword-based input filters.

The framework incorporates "pictorial code contextualization," disguising malicious intent within visual inputs presented in a programming context, leveraging the model's propensity to process code-like instructions to bypass runtime monitoring.
paper_summary.summary: PiCo, a framework developed by researchers from Wuhan University, Peking University, and BAAI, demonstrates a method for jailbreaking Multimodal Large Language Models by exploiting weaknesses in visual modality integration and code processing. It achieved an average attack success rate of 84.13% on Gemini-Pro Vision and 52.66% on GPT-4o, significantly surpassing previous methods.

title: PiCo: Jailbreaking Multimodal Large Language Models via Pictorial Code Contextualization

abstract: Large vision-language models (VLMs) such as GPT-4 have achieved unprecedented performance in response generation, especially with visual inputs, enabling more creative and adaptable interaction than large language models such as ChatGPT. Nonetheless, multimodal generation exacerbates safety concerns, since adversaries may successfully evade the entire system by subtly manipulating the most vulnerable modality (e.g., vision). To this end, we propose evaluating the robustness of open-source large VLMs in the most realistic and high-risk setting, where adversaries have only black-box system access and seek to deceive the model into returning the targeted responses. In particular, we first craft targeted adversarial examples against pretrained models such as CLIP and BLIP, and then transfer these adversarial examples to other VLMs such as MiniGPT-4, LLaVA, UniDiffuser, BLIP-2, and Img2Prompt. In addition, we observe that black-box queries on these VLMs can further improve the effectiveness of targeted evasion, resulting in a surprisingly high success rate for generating targeted responses. Our findings provide a quantitative understanding regarding the adversarial vulnerability of large VLMs and call for a more thorough examination of their potential security flaws before deployment in practice. Code is at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Singapore University of Technology and Design

keyInsights: Large, state-of-the-art VLMs are highly vulnerable to targeted adversarial attacks delivered via human-imperceptible visual perturbations, even when operating under black-box access.

The 'Matching Image-Image Features' (MF-ii) transfer attack strategy demonstrated superior black-box transferability compared to direct 'Matching Image-Text Features' (MF-it) for crafting adversarial examples.

Combining transfer-based initialization with query-based refinement (MF-ii + MF-tt) proved exceptionally effective, significantly boosting attack success rates and demonstrating that adversarial effects can propagate through multi-round VLM conversations.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are rapidly advancing, but their vulnerability to adversarial attacks, especially under realistic black-box conditions and for targeted malicious outputs, remains under-explored.

Previous research on VLM robustness often focused on smaller, conventional models with white-box access or untargeted attacks, failing to address the challenges of contemporary large, generative VLMs.

The multimodal nature of VLMs presents a risk where adversaries could exploit the visual input modality, known for its susceptibility to perturbations, to bypass textual safety mechanisms and compromise the entire system.
results: The combined MF-ii + MF-tt attack strategy achieved high CLIP scores, indicating significant semantic similarity between the VLM's generated text and the predefined target text, across models including BLIP-2, LLaVA, and MiniGPT-4.

Adversarial perturbations adhered to an L-infinity norm constraint of 8/255, rendering them imperceptible to human observers, and these isolated perturbations themselves lacked discernible semantic content.

GradCAM visualizations revealed that adversarial images redirected the VLM's attention maps, causing the model to focus on regions that visually aligned with the targeted concept rather than the original image content.
solution: A two-stage black-box adversarial attack methodology was developed, starting with transfer-based attacks for initial adversarial example generation.

The transfer-based approach employed 'Matching Image-Image Features' (MF-ii) using surrogate models, which generated a target image from the desired text and then crafted perturbations to align the input image's features with the target image's features.

These initial adversarial examples were then refined using a query-based strategy ('Matching Text-Text Features,' MF-tt) involving a Random Gradient-Free (RGF) method to optimize the VLM's output text similarity to the target text without internal model access.
paper_summary.summary: This study systematically evaluates the adversarial robustness of large vision-language models (VLMs) against black-box, targeted attacks. Researchers developed a combined transfer-based and query-based strategy that successfully induces models like MiniGPT-4 and LLaVA to generate specific, malicious text responses from imperceptibly perturbed images, with these effects persisting over multi-round conversations.

title: On Evaluating Adversarial Robustness of Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: The security concerns surrounding Large Language Models (LLMs) have been extensively explored, yet the safety of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remains understudied. In this paper, we observe that Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can be easily compromised by query-relevant images, as if the text query itself were malicious. To address this, we introduce MM-SafetyBench, a comprehensive framework designed for conducting safety-critical evaluations of MLLMs against such image-based manipulations. We have compiled a dataset comprising 13 scenarios, resulting in a total of 5,040 text-image pairs. Our analysis across 12 state-of-the-art models reveals that MLLMs are susceptible to breaches instigated by our approach, even when the equipped LLMs have been safety-aligned. In response, we propose a straightforward yet effective prompting strategy to enhance the resilience of MLLMs against these types of attacks. Our work underscores the need for a concerted effort to strengthen and enhance the safety measures of open-source MLLMs against potential malicious exploits. The resource is available at this https URL

keyInsights: MLLMs are highly susceptible to visual prompt attacks, even when their LLM backbones are safety-aligned, indicating a critical vulnerability in vision-language alignment.
Typography-based images are a potent attack vector, leveraging direct textual content within images to bypass safety filters more effectively than other visual cues.
The apparent 'safety' of some MLLMs often stems from a lack of generalizability or weak understanding rather than robust safety alignment or intelligent refusal mechanisms.
originalProblem: Safety issues of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remained significantly under-explored compared to the extensive research on unimodal LLM safety.
Existing MLLM benchmarks primarily focused on evaluating general capabilities and performance, lacking comprehensive tools for systematic safety evaluation.
MLLMs exhibited a specific vulnerability where query-relevant images could bypass text-based safety mechanisms, enabling the generation of harmful content.
results: All 12 evaluated state-of-the-art MLLMs demonstrated high susceptibility to visual prompt attacks.
Typography attacks significantly increased Attack Success Rate (ASR) for LLaVA-1.5-7B by over 30% and LLaVA-1.5-13B by over 28.3% compared to text-only baselines.
A simple safety prompt reduced LLaVA-1.5's ASR from approximately 77% to around 15% on the tiny benchmark version, showcasing its immediate effectiveness as a training-free mitigation.
solution: Developed MM-SafetyBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 5,040 text-image pairs across 13 harmful scenarios, generated using GPT-4 for questions and diverse methods for query-relevant images.

Introduced a 'visual prompt attack' strategy that translates textual key phrases into images (e.g., Stable Diffusion, typography) and integrates them into rephrased questions to activate MLLM vulnerabilities.
Proposed a straightforward 'safety prompting' defense mechanism, adding an explicit safety instruction before malicious queries to improve MLLM resilience.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark, MM-SafetyBench, evaluates the safety of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), demonstrating that current models are highly susceptible to visual prompt attacks using query-relevant images, even when text-based safety mechanisms are in place. The research also shows that a simple safety prompt can significantly reduce the success rate of these attacks.

title: MM-SafetyBench: A Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract:
Recently, there has been a surge of interest in integrating vision into Large
Language Models (LLMs), exemplified by Visual Language Models (VLMs) such as
Flamingo and GPT-4. This paper sheds light on the security and safety
implications of this trend. First, we underscore that the continuous and
high-dimensional nature of the visual input makes it a weak link against
adversarial attacks, representing an expanded attack surface of
vision-integrated LLMs. Second, we highlight that the versatility of LLMs also
presents visual attackers with a wider array of achievable adversarial
objectives, extending the implications of security failures beyond mere
misclassification. As an illustration, we present a case study in which we
exploit visual adversarial examples to circumvent the safety guardrail of
aligned LLMs with integrated vision. Intriguingly, we discover that a single
visual adversarial example can universally jailbreak an aligned LLM, compelling
it to heed a wide range of harmful instructions that it otherwise would not)
and generate harmful content that transcends the narrow scope of a `few-shot'
derogatory corpus initially employed to optimize the adversarial example. Our
study underscores the escalating adversarial risks associated with the pursuit
of multimodality. Our findings also connect the long-studied adversarial
vulnerabilities of neural networks to the nascent field of AI alignment. The
presented attack suggests a fundamental adversarial challenge for AI alignment,
especially in light of the emerging trend toward multimodality in frontier
foundation models.

keyInsights: Multimodality in LLMs significantly expands the attack surface, making them more susceptible to adversarial manipulation than purely textual models.
Adversarial examples pose a fundamental and unresolved challenge to current AI alignment strategies, as even highly aligned VLMs remain vulnerable to visual jailbreaks.

A single, universally effective visual adversarial example can compel an aligned VLM to generate a wide array of harmful content, including instructions not explicitly targeted during optimization, highlighting a potent and scalable attack vector.
originalProblem: Integrating vision into Large Language Models (LLMs) to create Visual Language Models (VLMs) expands the attack surface for adversarial manipulation due to the continuous nature of visual inputs.

Current AI alignment techniques for LLMs, such as instruction tuning and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), may not robustly extend to multimodal inputs, leaving vulnerabilities unaddressed.
Existing LLM jailbreaking methods predominantly focus on textual prompts, leaving the potential for cross-modal attacks through visual inputs unexplored.
results: Visual adversarial examples drastically increased the VLM's (MiniGPT-4) susceptibility to harmful instructions, with identity attack success rates surging from 26.2% to 78.5% and violence/crime from 50.1% to 87.3%.
Visual attacks demonstrated higher effectiveness and computational efficiency compared to text-based adversarial attacks, achieving lower loss values with 12 times less computational effort.

The attacks consistently bypassed safety guardrails across all tested VLMs, including the highly aligned LLaVA, and exhibited black-box transferability, allowing adversarial images optimized on one VLM to impact others.
solution: A cross-modal jailbreaking attack was developed using visual adversarial examples, which act as a "malicious prompt" to exploit VLM vulnerabilities.
The adversarial images were optimized using Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) on a small, few-shot corpus of 66 derogatory sentences to maximize the VLM's probability of generating harmful content.
The attacks were evaluated on three open-source, safety-aligned VLMs: MiniGPT-4, InstructBLIP, and LLaVA (LLaMA-2-13B-Chat based), and compared against text-based adversarial attacks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed visual adversarial examples that can universally bypass the safety guardrails of aligned Visual Language Models (VLMs), compelling them to generate harmful content across various categories, even when optimized on a narrow corpus. The method significantly increased harmful outputs on tested VLMs, including the highly aligned LLaMA-2-based LLaVA, and proved more potent and efficient than text-based attacks.

title: Visual Adversarial Examples Jailbreak Aligned Large Language Models

abstract: By integrating language understanding with perceptual modalities such as images, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) constitute a critical substrate for modern AI systems, particularly intelligent agents operating in open and interactive environments. However, their increasing accessibility also raises heightened risks of misuse, such as generating harmful or unsafe content. To mitigate these risks, alignment techniques are commonly applied to align model behavior with human values. Despite these efforts, recent studies have shown that jailbreak attacks can circumvent alignment and elicit unsafe outputs. Currently, most existing jailbreak methods are tailored for open-source models and exhibit limited effectiveness against commercial MLLM-integrated systems, which often employ additional filters. These filters can detect and prevent malicious input and output content, significantly reducing jailbreak threats. In this paper, we reveal that the success of these safety filters heavily relies on a critical assumption that malicious content must be explicitly visible in either the input or the output. This assumption, while often valid for traditional LLM-integrated systems, breaks down in MLLM-integrated systems, where attackers can leverage multiple modalities to conceal adversarial intent, leading to a false sense of security in existing MLLM-integrated systems. To challenge this assumption, we propose Odysseus, a novel jailbreak paradigm that introduces dual steganography to covertly embed malicious queries and responses into benign-looking images. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our Odysseus successfully jailbreaks several pioneering and realistic MLLM-integrated systems, achieving up to 99% attack success rate. It exposes a fundamental blind spot in existing defenses, and calls for rethinking cross-modal security in MLLM-integrated systems.

keyInsights: Current commercial MLLM safety filters are fundamentally vulnerable to attacks that conceal malicious content implicitly within non-textual modalities, as they primarily focus on explicitly visible threats.

The combination of dual steganography and MLLM function calling creates an unmonitored communication channel, enabling models to process and generate policy-violating content without direct exposure to safety mechanisms.

Redundancy injection, such as Hamming codes, is crucial for maintaining the fidelity and successful recovery of hidden messages even when images undergo noise and transformations during the MLLM interaction.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM jailbreak methods are largely ineffective against commercial MLLM-integrated systems due to robust explicit input and output safety filters.
Commercial MLLM safety filters rely on the assumption that malicious content must be explicitly visible, creating a fundamental blind spot for covert attacks.
The integration of MLLMs with external function calling mechanisms expands the attack surface, allowing for potential misuse if hidden instructions can be processed autonomously.
results: Odysseus achieved an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 78% across commercial MLLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.0, Grok-3) and benchmark datasets, significantly outperforming 10 state-of-the-art baselines, with some cases reaching up to 99% ASR.

The generated attack images demonstrated superior visual stealthiness (SSIM of 0.8361, PSNR of 68.5971), remaining imperceptible to humans and evading both OpenAI's Moderation API (0% detection) and state-of-the-art steganalysis detectors.

The attack maintained high extraction fidelity (92%-100% accuracy) for hidden content even under severe image transformations (e.g., JPEG compression, resizing, random noise), confirming robustness against real-world distortions and adaptive defenses.
solution: Odysseus employs a dual steganography paradigm, covertly embedding encrypted malicious queries into benign input images and extracting harmful responses from output images.
The method uses Base64 encryption, binarization, and Hamming codes to ensure robust, error-corrected embedding and extraction of content within images.
It leverages the MLLM's function calling capability to autonomously decode hidden malicious queries and re-encode harmful responses, bypassing explicit textual and visual safety filters.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Southeast University and Nanyang Technological University developed "Odysseus," a dual steganography method that covertly embeds and extracts malicious content in images to jailbreak commercial multimodal large language models. The technique achieved an average Attack Success Rate of 78% against leading MLLMs like GPT-4o and Gemini, bypassing explicit input/output safety filters by exploiting implicit communication channels.

title: Odysseus: Jailbreaking Commercial Multimodal LLM-integrated Systems via Dual Steganography

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly equipped with capabilities of
real-time web search and integrated with protocols like Model Context Protocol
(MCP). This extension could introduce new security vulnerabilities. We present
a systematic investigation of LLM vulnerabilities to hidden adversarial prompts
through malicious font injection in external resources like webpages, where
attackers manipulate code-to-glyph mapping to inject deceptive content which
are invisible to users. We evaluate two critical attack scenarios: (1)
"malicious content relay" and (2) "sensitive data leakage" through MCP-enabled
tools. Our experiments reveal that indirect prompts with injected malicious
font can bypass LLM safety mechanisms through external resources, achieving
varying success rates based on data sensitivity and prompt design. Our research
underscores the urgent need for enhanced security measures in LLM deployments
when processing external content.

keyInsights: Malicious font injection successfully manipulates LLM behavior by exploiting discrepancies between human visual perception and LLM interpretation of font files.

The effectiveness of font injection attacks is influenced by factors such as injection frequency, document format (PDF being more effective than HTML), and prompt placement (top placement is most effective).
Newer LLM models demonstrated higher susceptibility to these attacks, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP) introduces additional attack vectors for sensitive data exfiltration.
originalProblem: Traditional LLM security measures primarily focus on content-based filtering and semantic analysis, leaving a gap for attacks that exploit visual deception.
As Large Language Models increasingly interact with external data sources and tools, new security vulnerabilities emerge beyond text-only prompts.
There was no known attack vector that could hide adversarial prompts from human users while simultaneously exposing them to an LLM's visual processing capabilities.
results: Attack success rates improved with multiple hidden prompt injections, showing optimal effectiveness around 10-12 injection attempts.
PDF documents consistently outperformed HTML formats in attack effectiveness, attributed to more robust font embedding and better preservation of mapping corruptions.
All tested LLM models demonstrated some level of vulnerability, with newer model versions exhibiting higher susceptibility, and sensitive data leakage via MCP was achieved for various data types.
solution: The research introduces "malicious font injection," an attack vector that corrupts font code-to-glyph mappings, causing character codes to display different visual glyphs than expected.
Attackers embed malicious prompts in documents or web content using these modified fonts, making the prompts visually concealed from human users.
When LLMs process the document, they interpret the underlying character codes, effectively 'seeing' and processing the hidden adversarial instructions to manipulate responses or exfiltrate data.
paper_summary.summary: Xiong et al. identify "malicious font injection," a new attack vector that exploits LLMs' visual processing by embedding adversarial prompts within documents using corrupted font files. The technique enables hidden instructions to manipulate LLM responses and facilitate sensitive data leakage via protocols like the Model Context Protocol (MCP).

title: Invisible Prompts, Visible Threats: Malicious Font Injection in External  Resources for Large Language Models

abstract:
We propose a universal adversarial attack on multimodal Large Language Models
(LLMs) that leverages a single optimized image to override alignment safeguards
across diverse queries and even multiple models. By backpropagating through the
vision encoder and language head, we craft a synthetic image that forces the
model to respond with a targeted phrase (e.g., "Sure, here it is") or otherwise
unsafe content -- even for harmful prompts. In experiments on the SafeBench and
MM-SafetyBench benchmarks, our method achieves higher attack success rates than
existing baselines, including text-only universal prompts (e.g., up to 81% on
certain models). We further demonstrate cross-model universality by training on
several multimodal LLMs simultaneously. Additionally, a multi-answer variant of
our approach produces more natural-sounding (yet still malicious) responses.
These findings underscore critical vulnerabilities in current multimodal
alignment and call for more robust adversarial defenses. We will release code
and datasets under the Apache-2.0 license. Warning: some content generated by
Multimodal LLMs in this paper may be offensive.

keyInsights: Current MLLM safety alignment mechanisms are fundamentally susceptible to powerful, generalizable visual manipulation, indicating a systemic weakness rather than isolated vulnerabilities.

A single, carefully optimized visual perturbation can consistently override MLLM safety safeguards across a wide range of textual queries and demonstrate transferability to unseen model architectures.

Diversifying the target malicious responses during adversarial training (multi-answer supervision) significantly improves the attack's generalization capabilities and can lead to more varied, yet still unsafe, generated outputs.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have critical safety alignment mechanisms that are increasingly vulnerable to adversarial attacks.
Existing adversarial attacks often lack prompt universality, meaning they are specific to certain textual queries, or fail to transfer across different MLLM architectures.
The potential for a single, universal visual input to compromise MLLMs across numerous queries and models represents a significant, unaddressed security risk.
results: The proposed "Multi-Answer Attack" variant achieved substantially higher Attack Success Rates (ASRs) on safety benchmarks, reaching up to 81.3% on Phi-3.5-vision-instruct and 70.4% on Llama-3.2-11B-Vision-Instruct, outperforming all evaluated textual and multimodal baselines.

A single adversarial image, optimized simultaneously across four MLLMs, successfully demonstrated universal performance on each constituent model, confirming the feasibility of a potent universal attack against a known set of models.

Cross-model transferability was observed in leave-one-out experiments, where an image trained on a subset of models could still significantly increase the ASR on an unseen MLLM compared to its baseline safety performance.
solution: A gradient-based optimization pipeline generates a single synthetic adversarial image by backpropagating a masked cross-entropy loss from a target unsafe answer through the MLLM's entire vision and language components.

Prompt universality is achieved by training the adversarial image against a diverse dataset of both safe and harmful textual questions, forcing MLLMs to produce a fixed affirmative response regardless of the text query.

Strategies such as multi-answer supervision, Gaussian blurring, and localized perturbations are explored to enhance the attack's transferability, stealth, and ability to generate more natural-sounding malicious responses.
paper_summary.summary: A universal adversarial image was developed to bypass safety alignment in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), achieving high attack success rates (ASR) across diverse textual queries and demonstrating transferability to multiple MLLM architectures. This optimized single image can coerce MLLMs into generating specific unsafe responses, highlighting a systemic vulnerability in their refusal mechanisms.

title: Universal Adversarial Attack on Aligned Multimodal LLMs

abstract: The rapid development of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has exposed vulnerabilities to various adversarial attacks. This paper provides a comprehensive overview of jailbreaking research targeting both LLMs and MLLMs, highlighting recent advancements in evaluation benchmarks, attack techniques and defense strategies. Compared to the more advanced state of unimodal jailbreaking, multimodal domain remains underexplored. We summarize the limitations and potential research directions of multimodal jailbreaking, aiming to inspire future research and further enhance the robustness and security of MLLMs.
title: From LLMs to MLLMs: Exploring the Landscape of Multimodal Jailbreaking

abstract: Safety is critical to the usage of large language models (LLMs). Multiple techniques such as data filtering and supervised fine-tuning have been developed to strengthen LLM safety. However, currently known techniques presume that corpora used for safety alignment of LLMs are solely interpreted by semantics. This assumption, however, does not hold in real-world applications, which leads to severe vulnerabilities in LLMs. For example, users of forums often use ASCII art, a form of text-based art, to convey image information. In this paper, we propose a novel ASCII art-based jailbreak attack and introduce a comprehensive benchmark Vision-in-Text Challenge (ViTC) to evaluate the capabilities of LLMs in recognizing prompts that cannot be solely interpreted by semantics. We show that five SOTA LLMs (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, and Llama2) struggle to recognize prompts provided in the form of ASCII art. Based on this observation, we develop the jailbreak attack ArtPrompt, which leverages the poor performance of LLMs in recognizing ASCII art to bypass safety measures and elicit undesired behaviors from LLMs. ArtPrompt only requires black-box access to the victim LLMs, making it a practical attack. We evaluate ArtPrompt on five SOTA LLMs, and show that ArtPrompt can effectively and efficiently induce undesired behaviors from all five LLMs. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Current LLMs universally struggle with visually encoded text (ASCII art), demonstrating a "blind spot" for non-semantic information in their safety mechanisms.
Exploiting this deficiency, ArtPrompt provides an efficient and effective black-box jailbreak attack that outperforms existing methods.
Existing LLM defenses, such as perplexity-based detection and retokenization, are insufficient against this new class of visually-based attacks, highlighting a need for more robust safety alignment.
originalProblem: Current LLM safety alignment primarily assumes semantic interpretation of input text, overlooking non-semantic cues.
Existing jailbreak attacks largely focus on linguistic manipulation or token-level vulnerabilities.
LLMs lack the ability to effectively recognize or process information embedded visually within text, such as ASCII art.
results: LLMs like GPT-4 achieved only 25.19% accuracy on single ASCII character recognition and a mere 3.26% on character sequences in the VITC benchmark.
ArtPrompt achieved high attack success rates (e.g., 52% against Claude, 76% against Gemini), consistently outperforming five baseline jailbreak attacks.

ArtPrompt bypassed common defenses (perplexity-based detection, retokenization) and was partially resisted by paraphrasing; improving LLM ASCII art recognition via fine-tuning reduced attack success from 72% to 64%.
solution: Developed the Vision-in-Text Challenge (VITC) benchmark to quantify LLMs' ASCII art recognition capabilities.
Introduced ArtPrompt, a jailbreak attack that masks sensitive words in harmful instructions and replaces them with their ASCII art representations.
Evaluated ArtPrompt against state-of-the-art black-box LLMs and compared its effectiveness against five established jailbreak methods and common defenses.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Washington's Network Security Lab developed "ArtPrompt," a jailbreak attack that exploits Large Language Models' (LLMs) inability to interpret information conveyed through ASCII art. The study found leading LLMs achieved as low as 3.26% accuracy on a novel ASCII art recognition benchmark and that ArtPrompt successfully bypassed existing safety mechanisms with high attack success rates against models like Claude and Gemini.

title: ArtPrompt: ASCII Art-based Jailbreak Attacks against Aligned LLMs

abstract: With the emergence of strong vision language capabilities, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated tremendous potential for real-world applications. However, the security vulnerabilities exhibited by the visual modality pose significant challenges to deploying such models in open-world environments. Recent studies have successfully induced harmful responses from target MLLMs by encoding harmful textual semantics directly into visual inputs. However, in these approaches, the visual modality primarily serves as a trigger for unsafe behavior, often exhibiting semantic ambiguity and lacking grounding in realistic scenarios. In this work, we define a novel setting: vision-centric jailbreak, where visual information serves as a necessary component in constructing a complete and realistic jailbreak context. Building on this setting, we propose the VisCo (Visual Contextual) Attack. VisCo fabricates contextual dialogue using four distinct vision-focused strategies, dynamically generating auxiliary images when necessary to construct a vision-centric jailbreak scenario. To maximize attack effectiveness, it incorporates automatic toxicity obfuscation and semantic refinement to produce a final attack prompt that reliably triggers harmful responses from the target black-box MLLMs. Specifically, VisCo achieves a toxicity score of 4.78 and an Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 85% on MM-SafetyBench against GPT-4o, significantly outperforming the baseline, which achieves a toxicity score of 2.48 and an ASR of 22.2%. Code: this https URL.
title: Visual Contextual Attack: Jailbreaking MLLMs with Image-Driven Context Injection

abstract: With the significant advancement of Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs), concerns about their potential misuse and abuse have grown rapidly. Previous studies have highlighted VLMs' vulnerability to jailbreak attacks, where carefully crafted inputs can lead the model to produce content that violates ethical and legal standards. However, existing methods struggle against state-of-the-art VLMs like GPT-4o, due to the over-exposure of harmful content and lack of stealthy malicious guidance. In this work, we propose a novel jailbreak attack framework: Multi-Modal Linkage (MML) Attack. Drawing inspiration from cryptography, MML utilizes an encryption-decryption process across text and image modalities to mitigate over-exposure of malicious information. To align the model's output with malicious intent covertly, MML employs a technique called "evil alignment", framing the attack within a video game production scenario. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate MML's effectiveness. Specifically, MML jailbreaks GPT-4o with attack success rates of 97.80% on SafeBench, 98.81% on MM-SafeBench and 99.07% on HADES-Dataset. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Sophisticated jailbreak attacks can exploit the interplay between visual and linguistic modalities in VLMs to bypass safety filters.
Concealing malicious intent within images and subtly guiding the model through a 'virtual scenario' can trick VLMs into generating harmful content.
Current VLM safety alignments, even in advanced models like GPT-4o, are insufficient against multi-modal adversarial techniques that leverage cross-modal 'linkage'.
originalProblem: Existing Large Vision-Language Model (VLM) jailbreak methods frequently fail against advanced models like GPT-4o, creating a false sense of security.
Prior structure-based attacks over-expose harmful content in images, triggering VLM safety filters and leading to direct refusals.
Neutral text prompts in previous attacks often result in implicit rejections, where VLMs provide ethical advice or warnings instead of generating malicious content.
results: The Multi-Modal Linkage (MML) Attack achieved attack success rates (ASR) of 99.40% on SafeBench, 98.81% on MM-SafeBench, and 99.07% on HADES-Dataset against GPT-4o, substantially outperforming baselines.
MML demonstrated high ASRs on challenging topics like 'Illegal Activity' and 'Hate Speech' (over 95% with image mirroring), where baselines often achieved near 0% ASR.
The framework showed resilience against prompt-based defenses like AdaShield-Static, maintaining high ASRs (e.g., 87.80% overall with word replacement) while baseline attacks were largely nullified.
solution: The Multi-Modal Linkage (MML) Attack transforms malicious queries into typographic images and 'encrypts' them using methods like word replacement, image mirroring, rotation, or Base64 encoding to conceal harmful content.
The framework guides the VLM to 'decrypt' the hidden information using Chain of Thought (CoT) prompting, often providing a shuffled list of original words as a hint.

An 'evil alignment' strategy embeds the jailbreak within a virtual scenario (e.g., video game development) to covertly steer the VLM to generate detailed, malicious content by aligning with a 'villain's objectives'.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces the Multi-Modal Linkage (MML) Attack, a framework that leverages cross-modal encryption-decryption and a narrative-based 'evil alignment' to bypass safety mechanisms in advanced Vision-Language Models. The MML Attack demonstrates high success rates, achieving nearly 99% attack success against GPT-4o across multiple benchmarks by effectively concealing malicious intent and guiding the model towards harmful output.

title: Jailbreak Large Vision-Language Models Through Multi-Modal Linkage

abstract: With the rapid advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), securing these models against malicious inputs while aligning them with human values has emerged as a critical challenge. In this paper, we investigate an important and unexplored question of whether techniques that successfully jailbreak Large Language Models (LLMs) can be equally effective in jailbreaking MLLMs. To explore this issue, we introduce JailBreakV-28K, a pioneering benchmark designed to assess the transferability of LLM jailbreak techniques to MLLMs, thereby evaluating the robustness of MLLMs against diverse jailbreak attacks. Utilizing a dataset of 2, 000 malicious queries that is also proposed in this paper, we generate 20, 000 text-based jailbreak prompts using advanced jailbreak attacks on LLMs, alongside 8, 000 image-based jailbreak inputs from recent MLLMs jailbreak attacks, our comprehensive dataset includes 28, 000 test cases across a spectrum of adversarial scenarios. Our evaluation of 10 open-source MLLMs reveals a notably high Attack Success Rate (ASR) for attacks transferred from LLMs, highlighting a critical vulnerability in MLLMs that stems from their text-processing capabilities. Our findings underscore the urgent need for future research to address alignment vulnerabilities in MLLMs from both textual and visual inputs.

keyInsights: MLLMs inherit considerable safety vulnerabilities from their underlying Large Language Model components.
Text-based jailbreak attacks transferred from LLMs are notably more effective against MLLMs than existing image-based MLLM jailbreak methods.
The type of accompanying image has minimal influence on the success rate of text-based jailbreak attacks on MLLMs.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM alignment robustness research primarily focused on image-based jailbreak methods, leaving a gap in understanding textual vulnerabilities.
There was an unexplored question about the transferability of successful text-based Large Language Model (LLM) jailbreak techniques to Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
A comprehensive and large-scale benchmark was lacking to systematically assess MLLM vulnerabilities across both textual and visual modalities.
results: The JailBreakV-28K benchmark achieved an overall average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 44% across all tested Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
Text-based Large Language Model (LLM) transfer attacks showed an average ASR of 50.5% on MLLMs, outperforming image-based attacks (maximum 30% ASR).

Image inputs had a negligible effect on the success of text-based jailbreaks, with an average coefficient of variation of 9.0 among different image types, and MLLMs were most vulnerable to "Malware" and "Economic Harm" policies.
solution: Developed JailBreakV-28K, a comprehensive benchmark of 28,000 text-image pairs for evaluating MLLM robustness against jailbreak attacks.
Constructed RedTeam-2K, a meticulously curated dataset of 2,000 malicious queries categorized into 16 diverse safety policies.
Generated LLM-transfer attacks by pairing 5,000 highly effective LLM jailbreak prompts with diverse image types, alongside state-of-the-art MLLM-specific image-based attacks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers introduce JailBreakV-28K, a benchmark revealing that Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) inherit significant vulnerabilities from their Large Language Model (LLM) components, with text-based jailbreak attacks proving more effective than current image-based methods and largely unaffected by the accompanying image.

title: JailBreakV: A Benchmark for Assessing the Robustness of MultiModal Large Language Models against Jailbreak Attacks

abstract:
Recent advancements in Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have underscored
their superiority in various multimodal tasks. However, the adversarial
robustness of VLMs has not been fully explored. Existing methods mainly assess
robustness through unimodal adversarial attacks that perturb images, while
assuming inherent resilience against text-based attacks. Different from
existing attacks, in this work we propose a more comprehensive strategy that
jointly attacks both text and image modalities to exploit a broader spectrum of
vulnerability within VLMs. Specifically, we propose a dual optimization
objective aimed at guiding the model to generate affirmative responses with
high toxicity. Our attack method begins by optimizing an adversarial image
prefix from random noise to generate diverse harmful responses in the absence
of text input, thus imbuing the image with toxic semantics. Subsequently, an
adversarial text suffix is integrated and co-optimized with the adversarial
image prefix to maximize the probability of eliciting affirmative responses to
various harmful instructions. The discovered adversarial image prefix and text
suffix are collectively denoted as a Universal Master Key (UMK). When
integrated into various malicious queries, UMK can circumvent the alignment
defenses of VLMs and lead to the generation of objectionable content, known as
jailbreaks. The experimental results demonstrate that our universal attack
strategy can effectively jailbreak MiniGPT-4 with a 96% success rate,
highlighting the vulnerability of VLMs and the urgent need for new alignment
strategies.
title: White-box Multimodal Jailbreaks Against Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: The increasing sophistication of large vision-language models (LVLMs) has been accompanied by advances in safety alignment mechanisms designed to prevent harmful content generation. However, these defenses remain vulnerable to sophisticated adversarial attacks. Existing jailbreak methods typically rely on direct and semantically explicit prompts, overlooking subtle vulnerabilities in how LVLMs compose information over multiple reasoning steps. In this paper, we propose a novel and effective jailbreak framework inspired by Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) techniques from software security. Our approach decomposes a harmful instruction into a sequence of individually benign visual gadgets. A carefully engineered textual prompt directs the sequence of inputs, prompting the model to integrate the benign visual gadgets through its reasoning process to produce a coherent and harmful output. This makes the malicious intent emergent and difficult to detect from any single component. We validate our method through extensive experiments on established benchmarks including SafeBench and MM-SafetyBench, targeting popular LVLMs. Results show that our approach consistently and substantially outperforms existing baselines on state-of-the-art models, achieving near-perfect attack success rates (over 0.90 on SafeBench) and improving ASR by up to 0.39. Our findings reveal a critical and underexplored vulnerability that exploits the compositional reasoning abilities of LVLMs, highlighting the urgent need for defenses that secure the entire reasoning process.
title: PRISM: Programmatic Reasoning with Image Sequence Manipulation for LVLM Jailbreaking

abstract:
Fusing visual understanding into language generation, Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are revolutionizing visual-language applications. Yet, these models are often plagued by the hallucination problem, which involves generating inaccurate objects, attributes, and relationships that do not match the visual content. In this work, we delve into the internal attention mechanisms of MLLMs to reveal the underlying causes of hallucination, exposing the inherent vulnerabilities in the instruction-tuning process.
We propose a novel hallucination attack against MLLMs that exploits attention sink behaviors to trigger hallucinated content with minimal image-text relevance, posing a significant threat to critical downstream applications. Distinguished from previous adversarial methods that rely on fixed patterns, our approach generates dynamic, effective, and highly transferable visual adversarial inputs, without sacrificing the quality of model responses. Comprehensive experiments on 6 prominent MLLMs demonstrate the efficacy of our attack in compromising black-box MLLMs even with extensive mitigating mechanisms, as well as the promising results against cutting-edge commercial APIs, such as GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The 'attention sink' phenomenon, where certain tokens receive disproportionately high attention, disrupts normal information flow in MLLMs and can trigger content deviations from input images.
Image-text relevance in MLLM responses decreases as generation length increases, with this degradation corresponding to the emergence of attention sink tokens.

The proposed attack induces hallucinations with minimal input perturbations (as small as ", ""), yet produces significant fabricated content without degrading the overall quality or coherence of the generated responses.
originalProblem: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) frequently generate content that does not accurately reflect input images, a problem known as hallucination.
The hallucination problem undermines the reliability and trustworthiness of MLLMs, particularly in critical applications.
Existing defense mechanisms are largely ineffective against advanced hallucination inducement methods.
results: The attack successfully induced hallucinations across six prominent MLLMs (InstructBLIP, LLaVA-1.5, MiniGPT-4, Shikra, GPT-4o mini, Gemini 1.5 Flash) while maintaining the overall response quality (perplexity and GPT-4-assisted quality scores remained largely unchanged).
The attack demonstrated high transferability to black-box MLLMs and commercial APIs (GPT-4o mini and Gemini 1.5 Flash), reducing their question-answering accuracy.

Current defense mechanisms, including self-consistency verification, language-only mitigation, and LLM-based detection, were found to be ineffective against this attention-sink-based hallucination attack.
solution: Researchers developed an attack method that exploits the attention sink phenomenon by manipulating the model's internal attention mechanism to create artificial sink tokens.
The attack identifies optimal sink tokens, determines strategic injection points, and applies dynamic manipulation of hidden embeddings using minimal perturbations.
The methodology can be represented mathematically as a gradient-based perturbation to hidden embeddings, $h_{k}' = h_{k} + ", "",
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University and Alibaba Group developed 'Mirage in the Eyes,' an attack method that exploits the attention sink phenomenon in Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to induce hallucinations. The attack successfully generates fabricated content across various MLLMs, including commercial APIs, with minimal input perturbations while maintaining response quality, and demonstrated that current defense mechanisms are ineffective.

title: Mirage in the Eyes: Hallucination Attack on Multi-modal Large Language Models with Only Attention Sink

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have become powerful and widely adopted in some practical applications. However, recent research has revealed their vulnerability to multimodal jailbreak attacks, whereby the model can be induced to generate harmful content, leading to safety risks. Although most MLLMs have undergone safety alignment, recent research shows that the visual modality is still vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. In our work, we discover that by using flowcharts with partially harmful information, MLLMs can be induced to provide additional harmful details. Based on this, we propose a jailbreak attack method based on auto-generated flowcharts, FC-Attack. Specifically, FC-Attack first fine-tunes a pre-trained LLM to create a step-description generator based on benign datasets. The generator is then used to produce step descriptions corresponding to a harmful query, which are transformed into flowcharts in 3 different shapes (vertical, horizontal, and S-shaped) as visual prompts. These flowcharts are then combined with a benign textual prompt to execute the jailbreak attack on MLLMs. Our evaluations on Advbench show that FC-Attack attains an attack success rate of up to 96% via images and up to 78% via videos across multiple MLLMs. Additionally, we investigate factors affecting the attack performance, including the number of steps and the font styles in the flowcharts. We also find that FC-Attack can improve the jailbreak performance from 4% to 28% in Claude-3.5 by changing the font style. To mitigate the attack, we explore several defenses and find that AdaShield can largely reduce the jailbreak performance but with the cost of utility drop.

author_info.institution: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

title: FC-Attack: Jailbreaking Multimodal Large Language Models via Auto-Generated Flowcharts

abstract: Recent advances in multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have enabled unified perception-reasoning capabilities, yet these systems remain highly vulnerable to jailbreak attacks that bypass safety alignment and induce harmful behaviors. Existing benchmarks such as JailBreakV-28K, MM-SafetyBench, and HADES provide valuable insights into multi-modal vulnerabilities, but they typically focus on limited attack scenarios, lack standardized defense evaluation, and offer no unified, reproducible toolbox. To address these gaps, we introduce OmniSafeBench-MM, which is a comprehensive toolbox for multi-modal jailbreak attack-defense evaluation. OmniSafeBench-MM integrates 13 representative attack methods, 15 defense strategies, and a diverse dataset spanning 9 major risk domains and 50 fine-grained categories, structured across consultative, imperative, and declarative inquiry types to reflect realistic user intentions. Beyond data coverage, it establishes a three-dimensional evaluation protocol measuring (1) harmfulness, distinguished by a granular, multi-level scale ranging from low-impact individual harm to catastrophic societal threats, (2) intent alignment between responses and queries, and (3) response detail level, enabling nuanced safety-utility analysis. We conduct extensive experiments on 10 open-source and 8 closed-source MLLMs to reveal their vulnerability to multi-modal jailbreak. By unifying data, methodology, and evaluation into an open-source, reproducible platform, OmniSafeBench-MM provides a standardized foundation for future research. The code is released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Open-source MLLMs generally exhibit higher vulnerability to jailbreak attacks compared to closed-source commercial systems, with average ASRs of 37.5% versus 20.9%.

Attacks exploiting cross-modal dispersion (MML) and out-of-distribution inputs (CS-DJ) are particularly effective, achieving high attack success rates by circumventing safety filters through concealed malicious intent.
While some defenses (e.g., MLLM-Protector, CoCa) show broad effectiveness, specific safety fine-tuning (VLGuard) can inadvertently introduce new, exploitable weaknesses for different attack patterns.
originalProblem: Existing MLLM safety benchmarks focused on narrow attack types and lacked standardized protocols for evaluating defense mechanisms.
Prior datasets for multimodal jailbreak attacks offered limited coverage of diverse risk categories and user inquiry types, hindering comprehensive threat modeling.
Evaluations predominantly relied on a single metric, Attack Success Rate (ASR), which did not capture the nuanced severity of harm or safety-utility trade-offs in MLLM responses.
results: White-box attacks on models like MiniGPT-4 showed lower Attack Success Rates (e.g., JPS at 62.93%) when requiring sufficiently detailed harmful content (Avg-D mostly below 2.6).
Black-box attacks, such as MML, achieved high ASRs of 50.7% on Gemini-2.5 and 52.2% on Qwen3-VL-Plus by exploiting concealed semantic information.

The MLLM-Protector defense drastically reduced ASRs on GPT-4o (e.g., MML reduced to 0.27%), while the VLGuard on-model defense for LLaVA-1.5 significantly reduced ASR for HIMRD (from 20.40% to 1.20%) but slightly increased ASR for MML (from 0.93% to 1.73%).
solution: Researchers developed OmniSafeBench-MM, a unified, open-source benchmark and toolbox for multimodal jailbreak attack-defense evaluation.

The platform includes a newly generated, comprehensive dataset with 9 risk categories, 50 subcategories, and three distinct inquiry types (consultative, imperative, declarative) to capture varied user intentions.

It integrates 13 diverse attack methods (white-box and black-box) and 15 defense strategies (off-model and on-model) for standardized testing, along with a fine-grained, three-dimensional (Harmfulness, Intent Alignment, Level of Detail) evaluation protocol.
paper_summary.summary: OmniSafeBench-MM introduces a unified benchmark and toolbox for evaluating multimodal large language model (MLLM) jailbreak attacks and defenses, offering a comprehensive dataset, diverse attack/defense methods, and a multi-dimensional evaluation framework. Its extensive experiments on 18 MLLMs uncovered specific vulnerabilities and assessed the varying efficacy and trade-offs of 15 defense strategies.

title: OmniSafeBench-MM: A Unified Benchmark and Toolbox for Multimodal Jailbreak Attack-Defense Evaluation

abstract: We introduce new jailbreak attacks on vision language models (VLMs), which use aligned LLMs and are resilient to text-only jailbreak attacks. Specifically, we develop cross-modality attacks on alignment where we pair adversarial images going through the vision encoder with textual prompts to break the alignment of the language model. Our attacks employ a novel compositional strategy that combines an image, adversarially targeted towards toxic embeddings, with generic prompts to accomplish the jailbreak. Thus, the LLM draws the context to answer the generic prompt from the adversarial image. The generation of benign-appearing adversarial images leverages a novel embedding-space-based methodology, operating with no access to the LLM model. Instead, the attacks require access only to the vision encoder and utilize one of our four embedding space targeting strategies. By not requiring access to the LLM, the attacks lower the entry barrier for attackers, particularly when vision encoders such as CLIP are embedded in closed-source LLMs. The attacks achieve a high success rate across different VLMs, highlighting the risk of cross-modality alignment vulnerabilities, and the need for new alignment approaches for multi-modal models.

keyInsights: Vulnerabilities in Vision-Language Models can be effectively exploited through cross-modal interactions in their joint embedding space, particularly with only vision encoder access.

The "Modality Gap" in CLIP's embedding space causes image-based malicious triggers (e.g., OCR text, visual cues) to be significantly more effective than text-based triggers when generating adversarial images.

Adversarial images act as broad semantic cues, leading to persistent jailbreaks, context contamination in subsequent VLM interactions, and the activation of extreme biases beyond the initial harmful request.
originalProblem: Textual adversarial attacks on Large Language Models (LLMs) are becoming increasingly detectable and patched, reducing their effectiveness.

Existing multi-modal adversarial attacks on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) typically require white-box access to the entire model hierarchy, limiting their applicability to closed-source commercial VLMs.
VLM safety alignments often remain text-centric, leaving these models vulnerable to attacks exploiting non-textual inputs.
results: Image-based malicious triggers (OCR textual, visual, and combined) achieved high Attack Success Rates (ASRs), ranging from 84.9% to 87.0% on LLaVA and 60.4% to 63.3% on LLaMA-Adapter V2 across various prohibited categories.
Adversarial images optimized from purely textual triggers demonstrated minimal effectiveness, with average ASRs below 0.01%, confirming the impact of the modality gap.

Automatic toxicity classifiers corroborated human assessments, showing significantly higher toxicity scores for content generated via image-based triggers, indicating genuinely harmful and unsafe outputs from the VLMs.
solution: The method decomposes malicious prompts into a benign textual instruction and a hidden malicious trigger embedded within an adversarial image.
Adversarial images are generated by optimizing their embedding in a shared vision-language space to closely mimic harmful concepts, using only an off-the-shelf vision encoder like CLIP.
Four types of malicious triggers (textual, OCR textual, visual, and combined) are explored to craft adversarial images that, when paired with generic text prompts, bypass VLM safety alignments.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed "Jailbreak in Pieces," a compositional cross-modal adversarial attack method for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that manipulates image inputs in the aligned embedding space. This approach achieves high attack success rates, compelling VLMs to generate harmful content while requiring only black-box access to the language model component.

title: Jailbreak in pieces: Compositional Adversarial Attacks on Multi-Modal Language Models

abstract: As deep learning advances, Large Language Models (LLMs) and their multimodal counterparts, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), have shown exceptional performance in many real-world tasks. However, MLLMs face significant security challenges, such as jailbreak attacks, where attackers attempt to bypass the model's safety alignment to elicit harmful responses. The threat of jailbreak attacks on MLLMs arises from both the inherent vulnerabilities of LLMs and the multiple information channels that MLLMs process. While various attacks and defenses have been proposed, there is a notable gap in unified and comprehensive evaluations, as each method is evaluated on different dataset and metrics, making it impossible to compare the effectiveness of each method. To address this gap, we introduce \textit{MMJ-Bench}, a unified pipeline for evaluating jailbreak attacks and defense techniques for MLLMs. Through extensive experiments, we assess the effectiveness of various attack methods against SoTA MLLMs and evaluate the impact of defense mechanisms on both defense effectiveness and model utility for normal tasks. Our comprehensive evaluation contribute to the field by offering a unified and systematic evaluation framework and the first public-available benchmark for MLLM jailbreak research. We also demonstrate several insightful findings that highlights directions for future studies.
title: $\textit{MMJ-Bench}$: A Comprehensive Study on Jailbreak Attacks and Defenses for Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive performance and have been put into practical use in commercial applications, but they still have potential safety mechanism vulnerabilities. Jailbreak attacks are red teaming methods that aim to bypass safety mechanisms and discover MLLMs' potential risks. Existing MLLMs' jailbreak methods often bypass the model's safety mechanism through complex optimization methods or carefully designed image and text prompts. Despite achieving some progress, they have a low attack success rate on commercial closed-source MLLMs. Unlike previous research, we empirically find that there exists a Shuffle Inconsistency between MLLMs' comprehension ability and safety ability for the shuffled harmful instruction. That is, from the perspective of comprehension ability, MLLMs can understand the shuffled harmful text-image instructions well. However, they can be easily bypassed by the shuffled harmful instructions from the perspective of safety ability, leading to harmful responses. Then we innovatively propose a text-image jailbreak attack named SI-Attack. Specifically, to fully utilize the Shuffle Inconsistency and overcome the shuffle randomness, we apply a query-based black-box optimization method to select the most harmful shuffled inputs based on the feedback of the toxic judge model. A series of experiments show that SI-Attack can improve the attack's performance on three benchmarks. In particular, SI-Attack can obviously improve the attack success rate for commercial MLLMs such as GPT-4o or Claude-3.5-Sonnet.

keyInsights: MLLM safety mechanisms are sensitive to the structural integrity of inputs, diverging from core comprehension abilities when inputs are reordered.
Internal analysis of MLLMs revealed that while shallow layers process original and shuffled inputs similarly, deeper layers responsible for safety exhibit different behaviors for shuffled inputs.

Shuffling proved uniquely effective compared to other text and image mutation operations, suggesting it exploits a specific vulnerability in structural processing rather than general noise or semantic alteration.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) remain susceptible to 'jailbreak' attacks that bypass safety guardrails and induce harmful content.
Existing jailbreak methods often have low attack success rates, particularly against commercial, closed-source MLLMs employing additional outer safety filters.
There is a need to identify fundamental MLLM vulnerabilities that go beyond specific adversarial content designs.
results: SI-Attack significantly increased Attack Success Rates (ASR) against open-source MLLMs, boosting LLaVA-NEXT's ASR by 18.69% and InternVL-2's by 30.71% on MM-safetybench.
The attack achieved substantial ASR increases against commercial closed-source MLLMs, including GPT-4o (47.80% increase), Claude-3.5-Sonnet (39.70%), and Gemini-1.5-Pro (50.18%).

Both text and image shuffling contribute to the attack's effectiveness, with text shuffling often leading to a more pronounced increase in toxic scores, indicating a higher vulnerability on the textual side.
solution: The work exploits 'Shuffle Inconsistency,' a phenomenon where MLLMs maintain comprehension of harmful content in shuffled inputs, but their safety mechanisms fail to detect it.
A black-box, query-based attack called SI-Attack was developed, which iteratively shuffles words in text prompts and/or patch-wise blocks in images.
A toxic judge model (ChatGPT-3.5) provides feedback to guide the optimization process, searching for shuffled configurations that yield the most harmful MLLM responses.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Beihang University and Alibaba Group discovered a "Shuffle Inconsistency" vulnerability in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) where shuffling text or image components bypasses safety filters while preserving malicious intent. Their proposed query-based SI-Attack increased jailbreak success rates by up to 50% against leading open-source and commercial MLLMs.

title: Jailbreaking Multimodal Large Language Models via Shuffle Inconsistency

abstract: Despite efforts to align large language models to produce harmless responses, they are still vulnerable to jailbreak prompts that elicit unrestricted behaviour. In this work, we investigate persona modulation as a black-box jailbreaking method to steer a target model to take on personalities that are willing to comply with harmful instructions. Rather than manually crafting prompts for each persona, we automate the generation of jailbreaks using a language model assistant. We demonstrate a range of harmful completions made possible by persona modulation, including detailed instructions for synthesising methamphetamine, building a bomb, and laundering money. These automated attacks achieve a harmful completion rate of 42.5% in GPT-4, which is 185 times larger than before modulation (0.23%). These prompts also transfer to Claude 2 and Vicuna with harmful completion rates of 61.0% and 35.9%, respectively. Our work reveals yet another vulnerability in commercial large language models and highlights the need for more comprehensive safeguards.
title: Scalable and Transferable Black-Box Jailbreaks for Language Models via Persona Modulation

abstract:
Vision Large Language Models (VLLMs) are increasingly deployed to offer
advanced capabilities on inputs comprising both text and images. While prior
research has shown that adversarial attacks can transfer from open-source to
proprietary black-box models in text-only and vision-only contexts, the extent
and effectiveness of such vulnerabilities remain underexplored for VLLMs. We
present a comprehensive analysis demonstrating that targeted adversarial
examples are highly transferable to widely-used proprietary VLLMs such as
GPT-4o, Claude, and Gemini. We show that attackers can craft perturbations to
induce specific attacker-chosen interpretations of visual information, such as
misinterpreting hazardous content as safe, overlooking sensitive or restricted
material, or generating detailed incorrect responses aligned with the
attacker's intent. Furthermore, we discover that universal perturbations --
modifications applicable to a wide set of images -- can consistently induce
these misinterpretations across multiple proprietary VLLMs. Our experimental
results on object recognition, visual question answering, and image captioning
show that this vulnerability is common across current state-of-the-art models,
and underscore an urgent need for robust mitigations to ensure the safe and
secure deployment of VLLMs.

keyInsights: Proprietary VLLMs are highly vulnerable to transferred adversarial examples despite limited access to their internals
Model ensembling and contrastive learning significantly enhance attack transferability
Universal perturbations can consistently induce targeted misinterpretations across multiple VLLMs
originalProblem: Existing adversarial attacks on vision-language models focus on white-box settings or achieve limited success in black-box scenarios
The vulnerability of proprietary VLLMs to transferable adversarial attacks remains poorly understood, especially for targeted misinterpretations
results: Over 95% targeted attack success rate on GPT-4V compared to 45% in previous work
Successful attacks across object recognition, visual QA, and text recognition tasks
Demonstrated vulnerability of multiple proprietary VLLMs including Claude and Gemini
Identified key factors contributing to attack transferability through ablation studies
solution: Develop a transfer-based attack framework using ensembles of open-source models
Employ visual contrastive learning and data augmentation to improve attack transferability
Create universal perturbations that generalize across different images and models
paper_summary.summary: Carnegie Mellon researchers demonstrate that adversarial image perturbations crafted using open-source models can successfully induce targeted misinterpretations in proprietary vision-language models like GPT-4V and Claude, achieving over 95% attack success rates through a novel transfer-based framework combining model ensembling and contrastive learning techniques.

title: Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Black-Box Vision-Language Models

abstract:
The emergence of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLRMs) has enabled
sophisticated visual reasoning capabilities by integrating reinforcement
learning and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) supervision. However, while these enhanced
reasoning capabilities improve performance, they also introduce new and
underexplored safety risks. In this work, we systematically investigate the
security implications of advanced visual reasoning in MLRMs. Our analysis
reveals a fundamental trade-off: as visual reasoning improves, models become
more vulnerable to jailbreak attacks. Motivated by this critical finding, we
introduce VisCRA (Visual Chain Reasoning Attack), a novel jailbreak framework
that exploits the visual reasoning chains to bypass safety mechanisms. VisCRA
combines targeted visual attention masking with a two-stage reasoning induction
strategy to precisely control harmful outputs. Extensive experiments
demonstrate VisCRA's significant effectiveness, achieving high attack success
rates on leading closed-source MLRMs: 76.48% on Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking,
68.56% on QvQ-Max, and 56.60% on GPT-4o. Our findings highlight a critical
insight: the very capability that empowers MLRMs -- their visual reasoning --
can also serve as an attack vector, posing significant security risks.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: VisCRA: A Visual Chain Reasoning Attack for Jailbreaking Multimodal  Large Language Models

abstract: Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) have demonstrated the ability to interact with humans under real-world conditions by combining Large Language Models (LLMs) and modality encoders to align multimodal information (visual and auditory) with text. However, such models raise new safety challenges of whether models that are safety-aligned on text also exhibit consistent safeguards for multimodal inputs. Despite recent safety-alignment research on vision LMMs, the safety of audio LMMs remains under-explored. In this work, we comprehensively red team the safety of five advanced audio LMMs under three settings: (i) harmful questions in both audio and text formats, (ii) harmful questions in text format accompanied by distracting non-speech audio, and (iii) speech-specific jailbreaks. Our results under these settings demonstrate that open-source audio LMMs suffer an average attack success rate of 69.14% on harmful audio questions, and exhibit safety vulnerabilities when distracted with non-speech audio noise. Our speech-specific jailbreaks on Gemini-1.5-Pro achieve an attack success rate of 70.67% on the harmful query benchmark. We provide insights on what could cause these reported safety-misalignments. Warning: this paper contains offensive examples.

keyInsights: Audio input serves as an "Achilles’ Heel" for many current LMMs, leading to substantial safety vulnerabilities.
Multimodal adaptation can significantly deteriorate existing text-based safety alignments in LMMs.
Sophisticated audio-based adversarial attacks can bypass robust safety mechanisms of even highly-aligned proprietary models like Gemini-1.5-Pro.
originalProblem: Uncertainty whether safety alignment strategies for text-only Large Language Models (LLMs) translate effectively to multimodal inputs, particularly audio.
A significant lack of comprehensive red-teaming efforts specifically for the safety of audio Large Multimodal Models (LMMs).
Concerns about potential new vulnerabilities emerging when LMMs process and interact via audio.
results: Open-source audio LMMs exhibited an average Attack Success Rate (ASR) of 69.14% on harmful audio questions, showing an average safety drop of 45.15% compared to their LLM backbones.
Non-speech audio (silence, random noise) significantly impacted safety alignment, with Qwen-Audio showing up to 32.58% ASR variation.
A novel speech-specific jailbreak achieved a 70.67% ASR against Gemini-1.5-Pro by concealing harmful intent through spelled-out audio letters and prompt engineering.
solution: Systematic red-teaming of five audio LMMs (Qwen-Audio, Qwen2-Audio, SALMONN-7B/13B, Gemini-1.5-Pro) using a harmful question dataset.
Evaluation across three settings: harmful questions in audio/text, impact of distracting non-speech audio, and speech-specific jailbreaks.
Introduction of a novel speech-specific jailbreak strategy that decomposes harmful words into spelled-out letters in audio combined with a sophisticated text prompt.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Monash University systematically red-teamed audio Large Multimodal Models (LMMs), revealing that audio inputs are a key vulnerability. Open-source LMMs showed an average attack success rate of 69.14% for harmful audio queries, and a novel speech-specific jailbreak achieved a 70.67% success rate against Gemini-1.5-Pro.

title: Audio Is the Achilles' Heel: Red Teaming Audio Large Multimodal Models

abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are increasingly equipped with robust safety safeguards to prevent responses to harmful or disallowed prompts. However, these defenses often focus on analyzing explicit textual inputs or relevant visual scenes. In this work, we introduce Text-DJ, a novel jailbreak attack that bypasses these safeguards by exploiting the model's Optical Character Recognition (OCR) capability. Our methodology consists of three stages. First, we decompose a single harmful query into multiple and semantically related but more benign sub-queries. Second, we pick a set of distraction queries that are maximally irrelevant to the harmful query. Third, we present all decomposed sub-queries and distraction queries to the LVLM simultaneously as a grid of images, with the position of the sub-queries being middle within the grid. We demonstrate that this method successfully circumvents the safety alignment of state-of-the-art LVLMs. We argue this attack succeeds by (1) converting text-based prompts into images, bypassing standard text-based filters, and (2) inducing distractions, where the model's safety protocols fail to link the scattered sub-queries within a high number of irrelevant queries. Overall, our findings expose a critical vulnerability in LVLMs' OCR capabilities that are not robust to dispersed, multi-image adversarial inputs, highlighting the need for defenses for fragmented multimodal inputs.

keyInsights: LVLM safety alignments are vulnerable to attacks that exploit their OCR capabilities, as converting textual prompts into images forces reliance on visual processing, circumventing robust text-based safety filters.

Semantic distraction, achieved by fragmenting malicious queries and surrounding them with numerous irrelevant ones, significantly increases attack success by inducing cognitive load and preventing coherent detection of harmful intent.

The effectiveness of Text-DJ is primarily driven by the OCR-to-LLM pipeline and semantic processing, showing insensitivity to visual parameters like image arrangement, resolution, or font, as long as text readability is maintained.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) exhibit significant safety and security concerns, potentially generating harmful content due to vast, unfiltered training datasets.
Existing LVLM jailbreaking techniques are often white-box, model-specific, or computationally expensive, making them impractical for widely deployed, closed-source models.

Current LVLM safety alignments often overlook vulnerabilities where malicious text is embedded within images and fragmented across multiple multimodal inputs, failing to adequately scrutinize OCR-extracted text.
results: Text-DJ consistently achieves higher Attack Success Rates (ASR) compared to HADES and CS-DJ across various open-source (Qwen3-VL) and closed-source (GPT-4o mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash) LVLMs, often reaching 50-70% for specific harmful categories.
The method demonstrates strong robustness against external guard models, achieving a 0.00% refusal rate from the OpenAI Moderation API and maintaining high ASR even when filtered by GuardReasoner-VL.

Ablation studies confirm that Text-in-Image conversion is crucial (e.g., ASR drop from ~48% to ~20% without it), query decomposition to multiple sub-queries, and the use of semantically unrelated distraction queries are essential for the attack's high effectiveness.
solution: The Text-DJ method decomposes a harmful query into multiple, subtle sub-queries using a prompted Large Language Model to make individual components appear innocuous.
A set of semantically diverse and irrelevant distraction queries are constructed from an offline dataset to overwhelm the LVLM's safety protocols.

All sub-queries and distraction queries are converted into separate images (Text-in-Image) with randomized colors, arranged in a grid, and presented to the LVLM with instructions to focus on the sub-queries, thereby exploiting OCR to bypass text-based safety filters.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UCLA developed Text-DJ, a black-box jailbreaking method for Large Vision-Language Models that leverages their Optical Character Recognition capabilities by converting harmful textual prompts into images.
